title: Jack D.
text:
I explored the Median Household Income by State from Historical Income Tables: Households from United States Census Bureau. Some data wrangling first.

Codelibrary(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(janitor)
library(DataExplorer)
# Read file as raw, without automatic headers
raw_data <- read_csv(
  "../data/raw/Median Household Income by State 1984 to 2024.csv",
  skip = 7,
  col_names = FALSE,
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

# We keep the messy first rows because we need them to identify the real headers.

# Check the first few rows
raw_data[1:4, 1:12]

# A tibble: 4 × 12
  X1           X2    X3    X4    X5    X6    X7    X8    X9    X10   X11   X12  
  <chr>        <chr> <chr> <chr> <chr> <chr> <chr> <chr> <chr> <chr> <chr> <chr>
1 State        2024  <NA>  2023  <NA>  2022  <NA>  2021  <NA>  2020… <NA>  2019 
2 <NA>         Medi… Stan… Medi… Stan… Medi… Stan… Medi… Stan… Medi… Stan… Medi…
3 United Stat… 83,7… 638   80,6… 385   74,5… 588   70,7… 368   68,0… 535   68,7…
4 Alabama      65,5… 5,862 60,6… 3,993 59,9… 1,934 56,9… 2,294 54,6… 2,563 56,2…




Code# Extract the first two rows
year_row <- as.character(unlist(raw_data[1, ]))
type_row <- as.character(unlist(raw_data[2, ]))




Code# Find the state column
state_col <- 1

# Find only the columns labeled "Median income"
median_cols <- which(type_row == "Median income")

# Keep State + median income columns only
Household_median <- raw_data[-c(1, 2), c(state_col, median_cols)]




Code# Rename columns
income_years <- year_row[median_cols]
colnames(Household_median) <- c("State", income_years)




CodeHousehold_median <- Household_median %>%
  mutate(
    across(-State, ~ as.numeric(gsub(",", "", .)))
  )



CodeHousehold_median$State[1] <- "United States (Median average)"

Household_median <- Household_median[1:52, ]
Household_median <- Household_median %>%
  select(-matches("\\((39|40)\\)$"))
colnames(Household_median) <- gsub("\\s*\\(.*\\)", "", colnames(Household_median))
head(Household_median)

# A tibble: 6 × 42
  State    `2024` `2023` `2022` `2021` `2020` `2019` `2018` `2017` `2016` `2015`
  <chr>     <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>
1 United …  83730  80610  74580  70780  68010  68700  63180  61370  59040  56520
2 Alabama   65560  60660  59910  56930  54690  56200  49940  51110  47220  44510
3 Alaska    91260  98190  89740  81130  74750  78390  68730  72230  75720  75110
4 Arizona   84700  82660  73450  70820  67090  70670  62280  61130  57100  52250
5 Arkansas  64840  63250  53980  50780  50780  54540  49780  48830  45910  42800
6 Califor… 100600  89870  85300  81580  77650  78100  70490  69760  66640  63640
# ℹ 31 more variables: `2014` <dbl>, `2013` <dbl>, `2012` <dbl>, `2011` <dbl>,
#   `2010` <dbl>, `2009` <dbl>, `2008` <dbl>, `2007` <dbl>, `2006` <dbl>,
#   `2005` <dbl>, `2004` <dbl>, `2003` <dbl>, `2002` <dbl>, `2001` <dbl>,

#   `1985` <dbl>, `1984` <dbl>


The actual EDA begins below.

CodeHousehold_median %>%
  summarise(across(where(is.numeric), list(
    mean = mean,
    median = median,
    min = min,
    max = max
  ), na.rm = TRUE))

# A tibble: 1 × 164
  `2024_mean` `2024_median` `2024_min` `2024_max` `2023_mean` `2023_median`

1    52      42                1                 41                   0

#   total_observations <int>, memory_usage <dbl>



Code# Remove State column for numeric analysis
numeric_df <- Household_median[, -1]

plot_histogram(numeric_df)

crumbs: A  Rent & Roster: Mapping Student Housing Affordability Across the U.S.

title: Appendix A — Rent & Roster: Mapping Student Housing Affordability Across the U.S.
text:
A.2 Short Project Description
This project investigates the feasibility of rental housing for college-age students (18-24 years) across U.S. states by analyzing the relationship between median rental prices and median incomes by age group. Using Zillow rental data and U.S. Census historical income tables, we will create interactive visualizations and rankings to identify which states offer the most affordable housing options for students and how housing cost burden varies by age.
crumbs: A  Rent & Roster: Mapping Student Housing Affordability Across the U.S.

title: Appendix A — Rent & Roster: Mapping Student Housing Affordability Across the U.S.
text:
A.3 Research Questions

Which U.S. states are most and least affordable for college-age renters (18-24), and how does this compare to older age groups?
What is the rent-to-income burden ratio for 18-24 year-olds by state, and how many states fall into “affordable” (<30%), “burdened” (30-50%), or “severely burdened” (>50%) categories?
How has the affordability gap between younger renters (18-24) and established adults (35-44) changed over recent years, and which states show the most significant generational disparities in housing costs?
crumbs: A  Rent & Roster: Mapping Student Housing Affordability Across the U.S.

title: Appendix A — Rent & Roster: Mapping Student Housing Affordability Across the U.S.
section: A.4 Reasons and Inspirations Behind Choosing the Topic
text:
A.4 Reasons and Inspirations Behind Choosing the Topic

Personal relevance: All team members are currently students navigating housing affordability challenges
Policy importance: Student housing insecurity is a growing national issue affecting academic success and mental health
Data availability: Reliable, publicly available datasets (Zillow, Census) allow for reproducible, data-driven analysis
Visual impact: State-level comparisons create compelling maps and rankings that communicate findings effectively
Actionable insights: Results can inform prospective students, policymakers, and university administrators about regional affordability trends
crumbs: A  Rent & Roster: Mapping Student Housing Affordability Across the U.S.

Income by demographic characteristics
Annual (2000–2023)
National, State
Median income by age group (18-24, 25-34, 35-44, etc.)



Data Integration: We will merge Zillow state-level median rent data with Census state-level median income data by age group to calculate rent-to-income ratios and affordability categories.

While all team members are expected to contribute to the project, having leads helps with accountability.

Primary liaison with instructor/preceptors; schedules meetings; ensures deliverables are submitted on time; communicates team needs or issues


Technical Lead
Jack
Oversees all code development; ensures code functionality; leads data cleaning, merging, and visualization creation; maintains website/Quarto project


Research and Writing Lead

Leads literature review and background research; drafts written content for website; ensures clear communication of findings; edits final documentation and presentation materials

title: Appendix A — Rent & Roster: Mapping Student Housing Affordability Across the U.S.
text:
A.8 Conflict Resolution

Missed Deadline

Team member notifies Chat ASAP with reason and new estimated completion time
If delay affects downstream tasks, team redistributes work during next meeting
If pattern emerges (2+ missed deadlines), Elma schedules 1-on-1 to discuss support needs
Persistent issues escalated to instructor with team documentation



50/50 Split Decision
Elma has lead authority to initiate conflict resolution to ensure team progress 1. Each side presents argument with supporting evidence 2. Silent voting in Chat (majority wins) 3. If still deadlocked, consult instructor/preceptor for guidance 4. Document decision rationale in meeting notes


Code Disagreements
Jack (Technical Lead) has lead authority on implementation; Thusa and Elma can request documented explanation


Writing/Content Disagreements
Thusa (Writing Lead) has lead authority on narrative; technical accuracy verified by Jack and Elma
crumbs: A  Rent & Roster: Mapping Student Housing Affordability Across the U.S.

title: Appendix B — Case Study: How The Pudding Tells a Data Story
text:
B.1 Overview
For this case study, we looked at “Someone Clever Once Said Women Were Not Allowed Pockets” by Jan Diehm and Amber Thomas, published on The Pudding in August 2018. The piece asks a simple question most women already know the answer to: are the pockets in women’s jeans actually smaller than men’s, and by how much?
We chose this report because it does exactly what we want Rent & Roster to do: it takes something people already feel in their everyday lives and backs it up with real data in a way that’s easy to understand and hard to ignore.
crumbs: B  Case Study: How The Pudding Tells a Data Story

title: Appendix B — Case Study: How The Pudding Tells a Data Story
section: B.2 How the Story Is Structured
text:
B.2 How the Story Is Structured

1. It starts with a relatable moment
The story doesn’t open with statistics. It opens with the authors standing on a street in New York City, bags in hand, watching their male coworkers walk to dinner unburdened because their pockets actually work. It’s a small moment, but it immediately makes you think: yeah, that happens to me too.
We want Rent & Roster to open the same way: with the feeling of scrolling through Zillow as a student and realizing nothing is affordable, before we ever show a single chart.


2. The research question is specific and answerable
Instead of asking “why are women’s pockets so bad?”, the authors reframe it as something they can actually measure: how much smaller are women’s pockets, and are they functional at all? That shift from a vague complaint to a concrete, data-driven question is what makes the story work.
We tried to do the same thing with our research questions. Instead of asking “is housing unaffordable for students?”, we ask: what is the rent-to-income burden ratio by state, and how many states fall into the severely burdened category for 18 to 24 year-olds?


3. They’re upfront about how they got their data
There was no existing dataset for pocket sizes, so the authors went to stores in Nashville, New York, and Seattle and measured 80 pairs of jeans from 20 brands themselves. They explain exactly what they measured, how they controlled for size (all jeans had a 32-inch waistband), and where the data came from.
That kind of transparency is something we want to bring to our project too. We plan to clearly explain how we downloaded and cleaned the Zillow and Census data, what years we’re using, and how we calculate our burden ratios.


4. Visuals come first, numbers come second
The findings are shown through scroll-driven animations. You see all 80 pocket outlines before you read a single stat. Only after the visual sinks in do the numbers appear:

Women’s pockets are on average 48% shorter and 6.5% narrower than men’s
Only 40% of women’s front pockets can fit one of the top three smartphones
Most women’s pockets can’t even fit a hand past the knuckles

This approach makes the story accessible to anyone, not just people who are comfortable reading data tables. Our plan is to lead with the choropleth map of burden by state before diving into the numbers behind it.


5. It connects the data to something bigger
The story doesn’t end with measurements. It zooms out to show why pocket size actually matters: how limiting private space has historically been used to limit women’s freedom and mobility. The data stops being just an interesting finding and becomes evidence of something real.
That’s the move we want to make in our conclusions too. We want to connect rent burden ratios to what they actually mean for students: financial stress, limited college choices, and widening opportunity gaps by state.
crumbs: B  Case Study: How The Pudding Tells a Data Story

title: Appendix B — Case Study: How The Pudding Tells a Data Story
section: B.3 What We’re Taking From This
text:
B.3 What We’re Taking From This








Element
What The Pudding Did
What We’ll Do




Hook
Personal story before any data
Open with the student housing search experience


Research question
Vague complaint to measurable hypothesis
Specific burden thresholds (30%, 50%)


Data transparency
Documented every methodological choice
Document our Zillow/Census merge clearly


Visualization
Show first, quantify second
Choropleth map before tables


Big picture
Data as evidence of systemic inequality
Burden ratios as evidence of student opportunity gaps
crumbs: B  Case Study: How The Pudding Tells a Data Story

title: Appendix B — Case Study: How The Pudding Tells a Data Story
text:
B.4 References

Diehm, J., & Thomas, A. (2018). Someone clever once said women were not allowed pockets. The Pudding. https://pudding.cool/2018/08/pockets/
Thomas, A. (n.d.). Continue, pivot, or put it down. The Pudding. https://pudding.cool/process/pivot-continue-down/
FlowingData. (2018, August 16). A visual analysis of jean pockets and their lack of practicality. https://flowingdata.com/2018/08/16/a-visual-analysis-of-jean-pockets/
